This record separates the rescue and reported background from unresolved questions about the fatal use of force and does not treat its legality as judicially settled.

title: Sanchar Saathi mandatory-preinstallation order and rollback
status: Government order withdrawn after public and industry criticism
summary: The Department of Telecommunications directed smartphone manufacturers and importers to pre-install the government Sanchar Saathi cyber-safety app on new devices, distribute it through software updates for existing inventory, and keep it visible and functional. Privacy, consent, and user-control concerns followed, including criticism that the original direction restricted disabling or removing the app.
outcome: The Ministry of Communications announced on 3 December 2025 that mandatory pre-installation would be removed. The government said the app's growing acceptance made the mandate unnecessary; rights and industry critics treated the reversal as a response to privacy, consent, and implementation concerns. The app and the wider telecom-cybersecurity rules remained in place, so this record concerns the withdrawn mandate rather than the entire Sanchar Saathi programme.

title: Staff Selection Commission exam disruptions and aspirant protests
status: Administrative response; affected candidates and accountability questions remain
summary: Candidates reported technical failures, mouse and computer problems, distant or changed examination centres, and cancellations during SSC Selection Post Phase 13 after the Commission changed its examination vendor from TCS to Eduquity Career Technologies. Aspirants and teachers protested in Delhi and elsewhere; SSC acknowledged operational problems and said it was working with the vendor and C-DAC.
outcome: The SSC said the examination would not be cancelled wholesale but that a retest could be held for candidates who were denied a fair chance. SSC CGL examinations were later cancelled at multiple Delhi and Gurgaon centres for administrative reasons and rescheduled. The record does not adopt allegations that the vendor was inherently unfit as a proven finding; it records the reported disruptions, official response, and unresolved demands for transparent accountability.

title: Air India Flight AI171 crash in Ahmedabad
status: Fatal accident; preliminary report issued; final investigation pending
summary: Air India Boeing 787-8 Flight AI171 crashed shortly after takeoff from Ahmedabad while flying to London Gatwick. The aircraft had 242 people on board; the crash also caused deaths and injuries on the ground. The AAIB's preliminary report records the factual sequence and the continuing investigation.
outcome: The AAIB released a preliminary report on 12 July 2025 and continued its investigation with international technical support. The preliminary report is not a final causal or liability finding. This record therefore preserves the confirmed accident, the scale of loss, and the official investigation status without assigning blame before the final report.

title: Murshidabad violence during protests over the Waqf Amendment Act
status: Violence reported; court-ordered deployment and inquiries
summary: Protests against the Waqf (Amendment) Act turned violent in parts of Murshidabad district, West Bengal. Police and reporting recorded three deaths, injuries to police personnel, arson and vandalism, and displacement of families to neighbouring Malda.
outcome: The Calcutta High Court ordered deployment of central forces and directed governments to report on the situation. The NHRC took suo motu cognisance and announced an inquiry team; police arrests and competing claims about the response followed. The record does not attribute the violence to an entire community or treat political allegations as established findings.

title: Maha Kumbh crowd crush in Prayagraj
status: Fatal crowd disaster; judicial inquiry announced
summary: A crowd crush occurred at the Maha Kumbh festival in Prayagraj as pilgrims gathered for a major bathing day. Police reported 30 deaths and around 60 injuries; early reporting and witness accounts described confusion, dense crowds, barricades and rapidly changing casualty figures.
outcome: The Uttar Pradesh government announced a judicial inquiry and authorities continued rescue, identification and crowd-management operations. The official early toll and independent reports differed during the response, so this record preserves the reported figures and does not assign final responsibility for the disaster before the inquiry's findings.

title: New Delhi Railway Station crowd crush during Maha Kumbh travel rush
status: Fatal crowd disaster; high-level railway inquiry
summary: A crowd crush occurred at New Delhi Railway Station as passengers gathered to travel toward the Maha Kumbh festival. Reporting and authorities placed the death toll at 18, including children, with many more injured; the crush occurred around the footbridge and platforms 14 and 15 amid heavy passenger movement.
outcome: The Railways formed a two-member high-level committee, secured CCTV footage and called witnesses. Police notes and witness accounts examined crowd movement and possible platform-announcement confusion, while the ministry cautioned against treating leaked or preliminary accounts as its final finding. This record preserves the inquiry status rather than assigning final responsibility.

title: Cuddalore school-van and passenger-train collision
status: Fatal collision; statutory notice and investigation
summary: A passenger train collided with a school van at a level crossing in Cuddalore district, Tamil Nadu. Three children were killed and several people were injured. Initial accounts differed over how the crossing gate was being operated, so this record separates reported facts from unresolved responsibility.
outcome: The National Human Rights Commission took suo motu cognisance and sought reports from the Railway Board, Tamil Nadu's Chief Secretary and Director General of Police. The NHRC release also noted that a sanctioned underpass had not yet been constructed. The record remains an inquiry and public-safety failure entry, not a final finding of individual culpability.

title: Bengaluru RCB victory-celebration crowd crush
status: Fatal crowd disaster; judicial inquiry and criminal proceedings
summary: A crowd crush occurred outside M. Chinnaswamy Stadium during celebrations for Royal Challengers Bengaluru's IPL victory. The reported toll was 11 deaths and more than 50 injuries. The event raised questions about permission, crowd planning, communications and police deployment.
outcome: The Karnataka government ordered a magisterial inquiry, a later one-member judicial commission examined the lapses, and criminal cases and departmental proceedings followed. Court records describe the inquiry as examining the causes, sequence of events and possible negligence; this record distinguishes those proceedings from a final criminal conviction.

title: Gulzar Houz residential fire kills 17 in Hyderabad
status: Fatal fire; committee inquiry and court proceedings
summary: A fire in a mixed residential and commercial building near Gulzar Houz in Hyderabad killed 17 members of an extended family, including children. The preliminary fire account cited a short circuit, while other reported accounts raised questions about the building, escape conditions and the cause; responsibility remained under investigation.
outcome: The Telangana government formed a six-member IAS-IPS committee. Later court proceedings sought the state's response to alleged safety and regulatory lapses. This record preserves the death toll and inquiry status without treating a preliminary cause as a final finding.

title: Arpora nightclub fire exposes fire-safety and licensing failures
status: Fatal fire; magisterial inquiry and criminal investigation
summary: A fire at the Birch by Romeo Lane nightclub in Arpora, Goa killed 25 people and injured others. Initial reporting described a crowded venue, a narrow access route and questions about fire-safety equipment, emergency exits and licences.
outcome: The Goa government constituted a magisterial inquiry to examine the fire and responsibility. Police registered criminal cases, and subsequent reporting on the inquiry described alleged failures across the venue and public authorities. These are investigation and inquiry findings, not a final conviction of every person named in reports.

title: Dima Hasao illegal coal-mine flooding and prolonged rescue
status: Fatal mine disaster; multi-agency rescue and official review
summary: A rat-hole coal mine in the Umrangso area of Assam's Dima Hasao district flooded on 6 January 2025, trapping nine miners. The rescue operation involved state and central agencies and continued for weeks; official parliamentary material later recorded the deaths and recovery effort.
outcome: Rescuers recovered bodies after a 44-day operation. Parliamentary records described the mine as illegal and referred to the existing ban on rat-hole mining, while the National Disaster Response Force documented the operation. The entry records the regulatory and worker-safety context without claiming that every responsibility question has been adjudicated.

title: Piplodi government-school roof collapse kills seven children
status: Fatal public-school collapse; notices and accountability proceedings
summary: Part of a government school building in Piplodi village, Jhalawar district, Rajasthan collapsed during school hours, killing seven students and injuring more than 20 others. The cause and responsibility were investigated separately from the immediate rescue and compensation response.
outcome: The NHRC took suo motu cognisance and sought a detailed report from Rajasthan authorities on the collapse, injuries and compensation. Teachers were suspended and further safety audits and criminal or departmental proceedings were reported. This record does not treat suspension or an FIR as a conviction.

title: Sree Lairai Devi temple crowd crush in Goa
status: Fatal crowd disaster; fact-finding and magisterial inquiry
summary: A crowd crush during the annual Sree Lairai Devi Zatra at Shirgao in North Goa killed six people and injured dozens. Early accounts differed on the immediate trigger, while later reporting identified crowd management, narrow access routes and multiple queues as subjects of inquiry.
outcome: The Goa government ordered an inquiry and temporarily cancelled or postponed state-supported celebrations. A later fact-finding report described the incident as preventable and attributed lapses across crowd behaviour, the temple committee, district administration, police and local bodies; the report was disputed by the temple committee.

title: Srisailam Left Bank Canal tunnel collapse traps eight workers
status: Infrastructure collapse; prolonged rescue and recovery operation
summary: A section of the Srisailam Left Bank Canal tunnel near Domalapenta in Nagarkurnool district, Telangana collapsed on 22 February 2025, trapping eight workers. NDRF, Army, Navy, state agencies and specialist teams were mobilised during a prolonged operation.
outcome: The NDRF recorded the operation through 26 April, while later reports described the rescue being withdrawn after geological-risk advice. The record preserves the workers' trapped status, rescue effort and unresolved engineering or project-accountability questions rather than assigning an unproven cause.

title: 'Vote chori' allegations trigger a national election-integrity dispute
actors: Rahul Gandhi and Indian National Congress
status: Opposition claim; ECI rebuttal; evidence disputed
summary: Rahul Gandhi and the Congress alleged that voter-roll irregularities in Karnataka's Mahadevapura constituency and elsewhere showed systematic 'vote chori' and collusion benefiting the BJP. The Election Commission rejected the claims and said they required formal, sworn evidence rather than public assertions.
outcome: The Karnataka Chief Electoral Officer asked Gandhi to submit a signed declaration, identify the electors concerned and provide evidence so proceedings could be initiated. The dispute remained politically consequential but was not established as a judicial finding of large-scale election fraud in the cited record. The Bihar SIR litigation proceeded separately: the Supreme Court questioned the exercise's authority, procedure and timing and directed greater disclosure and access to documents.

title: West Bengal voter-roll revision becomes a live election-integrity crisis
status: Official roll revision; exclusion allegations contested
summary: Ahead of the 2026 West Bengal Assembly election, the Election Commission conducted a Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls. Opposition parties and affected voters alleged that eligible people, particularly Bengali-speaking and minority voters, were removed or made unable to vote. The Commission said the process targeted deceased, duplicate, shifted or otherwise ineligible entries and published a final roll of about 6.44 crore electors.
outcome: Polling proceeded in April 2026 and the results were declared in May. Reporting documented voters who said their names were missing despite valid documents, while the ECI maintained that the revision removed ineligible entries rather than targeting a political group. The record does not label the deletions as proven election fraud; it records a consequential voter-access dispute and the absence of a cited judicial finding that the election result was manufactured.

title: Haryana 2024 election 'vote chori' claim and ECI rebuttal
actors: Rahul Gandhi and Indian National Congress
status: Opposition claim; ECI rebuttal; no cited judicial finding
summary: Rahul Gandhi alleged that the BJP's victory in the 2024 Haryana Assembly election was enabled by large-scale voter-list manipulation, describing 25 lakh alleged bogus votes and presenting what he called the 'H files'. The allegations included duplicate or anomalous entries and were directed at the ECI and the BJP.
outcome: The ECI rejected the allegations as unfounded and said Congress had not filed objections or appeals against the electoral rolls during the relevant process. The cited reporting records a major credibility dispute, but does not establish the allegations as a court-proven finding or prove that the election result was manufactured.

title: EC orders a full Falta repoll after severe electoral offences
summary: After investigating polling complaints in West Bengal's Falta Assembly constituency, the Election Commission ordered fresh polling across all 285 polling stations. The order cited severe electoral offences, alleged deliberate interference with the poll process, and incomplete or unavailable footage in parts of the inquiry.
outcome: The ECI's decision to cancel the earlier poll and order a repoll is an official corrective action, not a finding that the entire West Bengal election was fraudulent. The record distinguishes confirmed electoral-process intervention in Falta from broader political allegations about EVM manipulation or the state-wide result.

title: West Bengal election petitions put disputed constituency results under court review
status: Petitions admitted or records preserved; merits unresolved
summary: The Calcutta High Court admitted an election petition challenging the Bhabanipur Assembly result, where Mamata Banerjee lost to BJP's Suvendu Adhikari. A separate published order in another West Bengal constituency directed preservation of voting and counting materials while allegations of corrupt practices, counting irregularities and statutory non-compliance were examined.
outcome: Admission of an election petition is not a finding that fraud or corrupt practice occurred. The proceedings preserve a legal route for testing constituency-specific allegations and require the record to distinguish pending judicial scrutiny from claims already established by a final judgment.

title: West Bengal post-poll violence brings arrests, FIRs and court scrutiny
status: Police cases and court directions; responsibility contested
summary: Violence, arson, vandalism and deaths were reported in parts of West Bengal after the 2026 Assembly results. Police said 200 cases had been registered and 433 people arrested in the first days after the result; BJP and TMC representatives blamed each other for attacks.
outcome: The ECI directed state authorities and security forces to take action, while the Calcutta High Court directed protection for people who had fled and arrangements for safe return regardless of party affiliation. The record does not assign collective guilt to either party and distinguishes reported arrests and court directions from final criminal findings.

title: Supreme Court declines plea to annul the 2025 Bihar Assembly election
status: Petition not entertained; allegations not adjudicated as proven fraud
summary: The Jan Suraaj Party asked the Supreme Court to annul the 2025 Bihar Assembly election, citing alleged electoral malpractice including alleged cash transfers during the election period. The court declined to entertain the plea and questioned whether a constitutional petition was the correct route to undo an electoral verdict.
outcome: The petition was withdrawn or not entertained rather than converted into a finding that the election was fraudulent. The record therefore preserves the allegation and the court response without presenting the claimed cash-transfer or malpractice allegations as established facts.

title: Tamil Nadu counting dispute leads to court-ordered preservation of election material
summary: A dispute over a Tamil Nadu Assembly result raised allegations of irregularities in vote counting and the handling of a postal ballot. The Madras High Court's proceedings treated preservation of vital electoral material as necessary while the dispute followed the legally available election route.
outcome: The court process did not establish fraud or invalidate the election in the cited record. It preserved evidence and separated a disputed counting claim from a broader conclusion about the state's election or any political party.

title: Aland voter-deletion investigation reaches chargesheet stage
status: SIT chargesheet; prosecution allegations not convictions
summary: A Karnataka CID Special Investigation Team investigated online requests seeking deletion of thousands of names from electoral rolls in Aland ahead of the 2023 Assembly election. Reporting on the investigation said 6,018 deletion requests were examined and 5,994 names were still associated with residents after verification. A later chargesheet named seven people, including a former BJP MLA, in the alleged operation.
outcome: The chargesheet and digital-evidence filings move the matter beyond a purely political allegation, but they do not establish guilt. The record keeps the accused status, investigative claims, and any later trial outcome separate and does not claim that the case proves nationwide election fraud or implicates the BJP as a whole.

title: Election rules restrict public access to electronic poll records
status: Rule change; transparency and privacy dispute
summary: The government amended Rule 93(2)(a) of the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961, limiting public inspection of certain electronic election records, including CCTV, webcasting and video material. The change followed a court direction concerning access to election documents and footage from the Haryana Assembly election.
outcome: The government and ECI position emphasised voter privacy, secrecy and misuse risks. Critics argued that restricting access could make independent verification of polling disputes harder. The record describes the rule change and its timing without claiming that it proves electoral fraud or invalidates any election.

title: AI deepfakes and campaign misinformation enter the 2024 election
summary: During the 2024 Lok Sabha campaign, manipulated audio and video became a documented election-integrity problem. The Election Commission directed recognised parties not to circulate deepfakes or patently false and misleading material, to remove such content within three hours of being notified, and to identify and warn the person responsible within the party. Independent reporting also documented AI-generated celebrity videos and other synthetic political media circulating during the campaign.
outcome: The advisory created a response rule, but it did not resolve every disputed video or establish that one party alone was responsible for the wider problem. This record tracks the documented technology and the regulator's response without turning campaign allegations into findings of electoral fraud.

title: Election Commission responds to rival complaints over campaign speeches
status: Notices and directions; political complaints contested
summary: For the first time in the 2024 campaign, the Election Commission sought a response from a party president over a complaint against a prime minister. Congress complained about Narendra Modi's Banswara speech, while the BJP complained about statements by Rahul Gandhi and Mallikarjun Kharge. The Commission later directed BJP campaigners to avoid communal appeals and Congress campaigners not to create an impression that the Constitution would be abolished.
outcome: The Commission's action was a regulatory intervention, not a judicial finding that either side committed a criminal offence or that the election was invalid. The episode remains relevant because it records both the complaints and the institution's choice to address them through party leadership rather than a public adjudication of each speech.

title: 2024 election returns Modi to office but ends the BJP's solo majority
summary: The BJP remained the largest party after the 2024 Lok Sabha election but won 240 of 543 seats, below the 272-seat majority mark. The BJP-led NDA crossed the majority threshold with its allies, and Narendra Modi was chosen to lead a third-term government.
outcome: The result preserved the incumbent coalition at the Centre while changing its parliamentary arithmetic: the BJP could no longer govern with a solo Lok Sabha majority and became more dependent on alliance partners. This entry records the result as political context, not as a positive or negative verdict on the government.

title: Form 17C voter-turnout data becomes an election-transparency dispute
summary: During the 2024 Lok Sabha election, civil-society group ADR and opposition politicians questioned the timing and format of voter-turnout figures, including revisions between preliminary and later percentages. ADR asked for scanned polling-station Form 17C records, which contain the authenticated number of votes recorded, to be published online after each phase. The Election Commission said Form 17C was available to candidates and agents and that there was no legal mandate to publish it on its website.
outcome: The Supreme Court declined to order the requested interim publication during the live election and adjourned the matter. The dispute did not establish that votes were altered or that the election was fraudulent; it exposed a transparency gap between records available to candidates and records readily available to the public.

title: ADR finds criminal cases declared by 251 newly elected MPs
status: Affidavit analysis; declarations are not convictions
summary: An Association for Democratic Reforms analysis of the affidavits of all 543 newly elected Lok Sabha MPs found that 251, or 46 percent, had declared criminal cases against themselves. It reported that 170 MPs, or 31 percent, had declared serious criminal cases and that 27 MPs had declared prior convictions. The analysis also reported a higher winning probability for candidates with declared criminal cases than for candidates with clean affidavits.
outcome: The figures describe self-declared affidavit information and do not establish guilt for pending cases. They provide a national accountability baseline for the 18th Lok Sabha; party-level comparisons should be read from the underlying ADR tables rather than converted into a claim that one party alone accounts for the problem.

title: Supreme Court rejects demand for 100% EVM-VVPAT verification
summary: The Supreme Court rejected petitions seeking a return to paper ballots and complete cross-verification of Electronic Voting Machine counts with VVPAT slips. The judgment accepted the existing system while directing additional safeguards, including allowing candidates to seek post-result verification of the burnt memory of a specified proportion of machines and requiring symbol-loading units to be sealed and preserved.
outcome: The judgment did not find EVM tampering or validate claims of election fraud. It settled the 2024 litigation against 100% verification while leaving a continuing public debate about how much independent audit access is appropriate for electronic elections.

title: Parliament changes the Election Commission appointment panel
status: Act in force; constitutional challenge pending
summary: Parliament enacted the Chief Election Commissioner and Other Election Commissioners (Appointment, Conditions of Service and Term of Office) Act, 2023. It replaced the Chief Justice of India on the selection committee with a Union Cabinet Minister nominated by the Prime Minister, while retaining the Prime Minister and the Leader of Opposition as the other members. The law followed the Supreme Court's March 2023 interim appointment framework, which had included the Chief Justice.
outcome: Supporters described the law as Parliament exercising its power under Article 324 and creating a statutory process. Critics argued that replacing the Chief Justice with a government-nominated minister increased executive influence over the election watchdog. The Supreme Court did not stay the law before the 2024 general election; the record does not treat the appointment structure alone as proof of an election being rigged.

title: Lok Sabha expels Mahua Moitra after Ethics Committee report
summary: The Lok Sabha adopted a motion expelling Trinamool Congress MP Mahua Moitra after its Ethics Committee found her conduct unethical in a complaint alleging that she shared parliamentary login credentials and accepted benefits in exchange for questions. The parliamentary record confirms the expulsion; the underlying cash-for-query allegation was contested and the committee recommended a separate government investigation into the alleged money trail.
outcome: The motion passed by voice vote after opposition members objected and walked out. Supporters of the action pointed to credential-sharing and parliamentary privilege; critics argued that the process was rushed, that Moitra was not allowed to make a full intervention in the House, and that the committee's findings did not itself establish a criminal conviction. This record distinguishes the expulsion from the unresolved criminal allegation.

title: Delhi services power dispute follows Supreme Court ruling
status: Act in force; constitutional challenge pending
summary: On 11 May 2023, the Supreme Court held that Delhi's elected government had control over services other than public order, police, and land. Eight days later, the Union government promulgated an ordinance creating the National Capital Civil Services Authority and shifting decisive power over postings and disciplinary matters toward the Lieutenant Governor. Parliament later enacted the Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi (Amendment) Act, 2023.
outcome: The Union government said the framework balanced Delhi's local administration with the national role of the capital. The Delhi government and critics said the ordinance and Act effectively reversed the Court's allocation of executive power and weakened elected accountability. The legal dispute was referred to a Constitution Bench; this record does not predetermine the constitutional outcome.

title: Delhi Chief Minister arrested before the general election, then granted interim bail
status: Arrested; interim bail granted; prosecution ongoing
summary: The Enforcement Directorate arrested Delhi Chief Minister Arvind Kejriwal on 21 March 2024 in the Delhi excise-policy money-laundering case, shortly before Lok Sabha polling began. Kejriwal denied the allegations and opposition parties called the timing politically motivated. On 10 May, the Supreme Court granted him interim bail until 1 June to campaign, while expressly leaving the merits of the arrest challenge unresolved and imposing conditions including surrender after the election period.
outcome: The arrest became a central election-period dispute over the use of federal investigative agencies against opposition leaders. The interim-bail order was temporary and did not amount to an acquittal or finding that the prosecution was false. The record therefore separates the confirmed arrest and court order from both the agency's allegations and the opposition's claim of political targeting.

title: Congress bank-account lien becomes an election-finance dispute
status: Tax dispute; competing accounts of account restrictions
summary: Ahead of the 2024 general election, the Congress party said the Income Tax Department had effectively frozen party and Youth Congress accounts over a demand of about Rs 2.1 billion. The Income Tax Department and BJP rejected the description of a blanket freeze, saying the action involved a lien or recovery connected to disputed tax treatment of political donations and late filing. Reporting recorded that the tribunal allowed partial operation while the dispute was heard and that money was recovered or attached.
outcome: The episode raised a major election-finance and level-playing-field dispute because it affected the principal opposition party during the campaign period. The record does not declare the tax demand unlawful or the party's allegation proven; it preserves the competing descriptions and the tribunal process.

title: PM CARES Fund transparency and RTI dispute
summary: The Union government established the PM CARES Fund during the COVID-19 emergency. RTI applicants and public-interest petitioners sought information about its structure, donations, and use of public institutional resources. The government said the fund was a public charitable trust and not a public authority under the RTI Act; the Supreme Court declined to order transfer of donations to the National Disaster Response Fund and recorded that PM CARES was not subject to CAG audit in the requested manner.
outcome: The fund published selected audited statements, while applicants and transparency advocates continued to challenge the limits on RTI access and public audit. Later court proceedings addressed disclosure of tax-exemption files. This record distinguishes the confirmed legal position in the cited orders from criticism about transparency and does not infer diversion or misuse without evidence.

title: RTI Amendment changes Information Commission terms and service conditions
summary: Parliament passed the Right to Information (Amendment) Act, 2019. It removed the RTI Act's fixed statutory terms and salary equivalence for the Central and State Information Commissioners, instead authorising the Central Government to prescribe their terms, salaries, allowances, and service conditions by rules.
outcome: The government said the amendment rationalised the service framework and did not weaken the RTI right itself. Opposition parties and transparency advocates argued that executive control over tenure and pay could reduce the institutional independence of bodies that decide disclosure disputes. PRS noted the change as a significant alteration to the commission structure; this record does not claim that every later RTI decision was politically controlled.

title: 146 opposition MPs suspended during Parliament's security-breach session
summary: During the Winter Session of Parliament, PRS reported that 146 opposition MPs were suspended, including 100 from the Lok Sabha and 46 from the Rajya Sabha. The suspensions followed disruptions and demands for a statement from the Home Minister after two intruders entered the Lok Sabha on 13 December. Parliament nevertheless passed the three criminal-law replacement bills and the Telecommunications Bill during the session.
outcome: The government and presiding officers treated the suspensions as responses to disorder; opposition parties said the scale of the action restricted scrutiny after the security breach. PRS reported that nearly 20% of MPs were suspended and that bills introduced in the session were passed without referral to committees. This record describes the parliamentary process and its dispute without declaring every bill invalid or treating suspension alone as proof of authoritarianism.

title: Police lathi-charge, tear gas at Sansad Chalo march near Parliament
summary: On 20 July 2026, the first day of the monsoon session, the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) led a 'Sansad Chalo' march from Jantar Mantar toward Parliament demanding the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan and reforms over the 2026 NEET paper leak and CBSE marking irregularities. Delhi Police had denied permission, imposed Section 163 BNSS across New Delhi district banning gatherings of four or more, closed metro stations, and readied water cannons. As protesters neared Parliament, police used lathi charges and tear gas to disperse close to 1,000 people, with some protesters shown bleeding from head and upper-body injuries in visuals circulated on social media.
outcome: Police detained CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke, Samajwadi Party MP Dimple Yadav, and several other protesters, and imposed an internet shutdown across a roughly 5-km radius of Jantar Mantar. Sonam Wangchuk had been forcibly hospitalised on 18 July during a related hunger strike. Inside Parliament, the first day of the monsoon session was adjourned after opposition parties raised the crackdown. Injury and detention totals were not officially confirmed.

title: Modi has never held a solo press conference in over 12 years as PM
summary: Prime Minister Narendra Modi has not held a single solo press conference — where reporters can freely ask questions — since assuming office on 26 May 2014. His sole appearance at anything resembling a press conference was on 17 May 2019, at BJP headquarters after the election result, where he sat silently while Amit Shah spoke and took no direct questions from journalists. Modi has given interviews to select, pre-screened media organisations, but no open, unscripted press interaction with the Indian press corps has ever occurred across more than 12 years in office. Multiple Indian and international media organisations have documented and reported on this pattern.
outcome: The pattern remains unchanged as of mid-2026. Media freedom groups including Reporters Without Borders have noted the inaccessibility of the Prime Minister to the domestic press as a factor in India's declining press freedom rankings. The government has not addressed the absence of press conferences.

title: Supreme Court rejects withdrawal of Kerala Assembly vandalism prosecution
summary: During the Kerala Assembly sitting on 13 March 2015, legislators damaged microphones and other House property amid disruption of the budget presentation. The state later sought to withdraw the criminal prosecution, invoking legislative privilege and public interest. In 2021, the Supreme Court held that privileges and immunities do not place alleged destruction of public property outside ordinary criminal law and rejected the withdrawal effort.
outcome: The Supreme Court dismissed the appeals on 28 July 2021, leaving the prosecution to proceed on its merits. The judgment did not itself convict the accused legislators.

title: Puttingal temple fireworks disaster kills 110 people
summary: An explosion during an unauthorised competitive fireworks display at Puttingal Devi Temple in Kollam district killed 110 people and injured 461, according to the Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation's 2016-17 annual report. The disaster exposed failures in licensing, explosives control, crowd safety and enforcement despite restrictions on the display.
outcome: PESO investigated the explosives-safety failures and the NHRC issued notice to the Kerala government. The casualty figures use the later PESO record rather than early, changing news totals.

title: Kerala floods trigger statewide recovery and preparedness audit
summary: Exceptionally heavy monsoon rainfall, flooding and landslides caused a statewide humanitarian and infrastructure disaster in August 2018. The post-disaster needs assessment documented extensive damage and recovery requirements across housing, infrastructure, livelihoods and public services. A later CAG performance audit examined preparedness and response and identified material weaknesses in flood-risk planning and institutional readiness.
outcome: The disaster produced a formal post-disaster needs assessment, reconstruction programme and CAG audit. The record does not reduce the disaster to a single cause or characterize the flooding as wholly man-made.

title: Mundakkai-Chooralmala landslides devastate Wayanad communities
summary: Multiple landslides struck the Mundakkai-Chooralmala area of Meppadi in Wayanad district on 30 July 2024, destroying settlements and triggering a large rescue, relief and recovery operation. Official state and Union reports document mass casualties, displacement, infrastructure loss and the scale of rehabilitation needs, but the public record contains changing counts across reporting dates.
outcome: Authorities declared the affected area a disaster zone and began a long-term rehabilitation and township process. This entry avoids combining bodies, body parts and missing-person reports into a false definitive death total.

title: Larji barrage release washes away 24 students and a tour conductor
summary: Twenty-four engineering students and one tour conductor were washed away in the Beas River near Thalout after discharge from the Larji barrage rose rapidly on 8 June 2014. A Union Home Ministry parliamentary answer records the gate opening and flow increase, recovery of the students' bodies and relief measures. The incident exposed failures in dam-release warnings, riverbed access control and public safety.
outcome: Police filed a charge-sheet against six power-project officials and three accompanying faculty members. In 2017, the Himachal Pradesh High Court refused to quash proceedings against the faculty members and left the merits for trial; this record does not claim a final conviction.

title: Kotkhai custodial death leads to convictions of eight police personnel
summary: After police detained suspects in the investigation of a minor's rape and murder in Kotkhai, one detainee, Suraj Singh, died in custody during the night of 18-19 July 2017. The Himachal Pradesh High Court transferred the rape-murder and custodial-death investigations to the CBI. The custodial-death case concerned senior police officers, alleged torture and alleged fabrication of the initial account.
outcome: A Special CBI Court in Chandigarh convicted Inspector General Zahur Haider Zaidi and seven other police personnel on 18 January 2025 and acquitted former superintendent of police D. W. Negi. The entry states the trial result without treating it as immune from appeal.

title: Recruitment paper leak leads Himachal government to abolish HPSSC
summary: A Vigilance investigation uncovered an alleged leak of the Junior Office Assistant (IT) recruitment examination paper scheduled for 25 December 2022. The new state government suspended HPSSC functioning on 26 December. It later said an inquiry found prima facie involvement of officials in multiple leaks, that two Vigilance FIRs had been registered and that an initial charge-sheet had been filed.
outcome: On 21 February 2023, the government dissolved HPSSC with immediate effect and transferred pending recruitment functions initially to the Himachal Pradesh Public Service Commission. Investigative allegations are not presented as blanket convictions of commission personnel.

title: Himachal monsoon disaster prompts statewide damage and recovery assessment
summary: Repeated heavy rainfall, floods, cloudbursts and landslides caused statewide loss of life and extensive damage during the 2023 monsoon. The state disaster authority's post-disaster needs assessment covered social, infrastructure and productive sectors. Its satellite assessment mapped 5,748 landslides across 45.44 square kilometres during the 1 June-15 September study window.
outcome: The state published a formal Post-Disaster Needs Assessment to structure recovery, reconstruction and financing. The season-wide record is not presented as a single landslide or a single-day casualty event.

title: Supreme Court reverses Arunachal Pradesh constitutional intervention
status: Constitutional actions quashed by Supreme Court
summary: A political and constitutional crisis followed the Governor's 9 December 2015 decision to advance an Assembly session and set its agenda, the removal of the Speaker, imposition of President's Rule and installation of a rival ministry. The dispute reached a Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court.
outcome: On 13 July 2016, the Supreme Court quashed the Governor's order and message, set aside consequential steps and restored the status quo ante as it existed before the unconstitutional intervention. Nabam Tuki was briefly restored before resigning on 16 July.

title: Khonsa ambush kills MLA Tirong Aboh and ten others
summary: Arunachal Pradesh MLA Tirong Aboh and ten other people were killed in an armed ambush near Khonsa in Tirap district on 21 May 2019. The attack occurred during the Assembly election period and was treated as a major insurgency-linked political assassination.
outcome: The National Investigation Agency registered RC-03/2019/NIA/GUW and took over the investigation. This entry uses the NIA's eleven-person total and does not assign criminal responsibility beyond the agency's public case record.

title: APPSC examination paper leak triggers cancellation and CBI investigation
status: Investigated; later Supreme Court disciplinary ruling
summary: Questions from the Arunachal Pradesh Public Service Commission's Assistant Engineer examination held on 26-27 August 2022 were allegedly leaked before the examination. A complaint led to a police case, cancellation of the examination and transfer of the investigation to the CBI in October 2022. The scandal raised systemic concerns about recruitment integrity, but liability must be assessed person by person.
outcome: In August 2025, the Supreme Court set aside disciplinary action against APPSC member Mepung Tadar Bage because the record did not establish her involvement in the leak. That ruling concerned one official and did not erase the underlying leak investigation.

title: Supreme Court orders CBI preliminary enquiry into Arunachal public works
status: Court-ordered preliminary enquiry; no merits finding
summary: The Supreme Court directed the CBI to conduct a preliminary enquiry into allegations concerning the award and execution of public works and contracts in Arunachal Pradesh during the period from 1 January 2015 to 31 December 2025. The order required access to state records and set a reporting timetable.
outcome: The Court directed a preliminary-enquiry report within sixteen weeks and expressly avoided deciding the allegations on their merits. The order is therefore recorded as an accountability process, not as a corruption conviction or factual finding against named political figures.

title: Aqua Marine tourist-boat sinking kills 22 near Port Blair
status: Fatal incident; investigation and safety audit ordered
summary: The private tourist boat MV Aqua Marine sank on the Ross Island-North Bay route near Port Blair while carrying 48 passengers and two crew members. A later Rajya Sabha record reconciled the casualty count at 22 deaths, with 26 passengers and both crew rescued, and reported that the boat was overloaded.
outcome: The Administration ordered a magisterial inquiry and safety audit of tourist boats; police registered an FIR and made arrests. The entry uses the later official 22-death count instead of early reports of 21 and does not claim a final conviction.

title: North Sentinel intrusion prompts tribal-protection review
status: Reported fatal intrusion; official protection review
summary: A US national illegally entered the prohibited North Sentinel tribal reserve and was reported killed. The Ministry of Home Affairs confirmed the reported killing in a parliamentary answer and restated that the island and five kilometres of surrounding coastal sea were closed to outsiders under the territory's tribal-protection regulation.
outcome: The National Commission for Scheduled Tribes reviewed how the landing occurred and recommended stronger patrols, public warnings and restoration of permit safeguards for islands inhabited by Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups. The body was not recovered in the cited record, so the manner of death is not presented as forensically established.

title: NGT leaves Great Nicobar project clearances in place under strict conditions
category: Environment, tribal safeguards and strategic infrastructure
actors: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
status: Clearances operative; NGT applications disposed with compliance direction
summary: The integrated Great Nicobar proposal covers a transshipment terminal, township development and a power project. In April 2023, the National Green Tribunal created a high-powered committee to revisit coral impacts, baseline data and coastal-regulation issues and restricted irreversible work while that review occurred.
outcome: On 16 February 2026, the NGT held that the committee had addressed the referred issues, found no ground to interfere, disposed of the applications and directed strict compliance with clearance conditions. The order does not establish zero environmental or tribal risk, and applicants' allegations are not treated as proved violations.

title: CAG finds Rs 22.14 crore revenue loss in Andaman shipping charges
category: Institutional accountability, shipping and public finance
status: Final CAG audit finding; corrective status not established
summary: CAG concluded that the Directorate of Shipping Services' failure to revise container-freight and related charges caused Rs 22.14 crore in lost revenue. A rate-revision proposal submitted in 2014 went undecided for six years, and a renewed 2021 proposal was still unapproved as of October 2022. The audit also identified non-realisation of auction deposits.
outcome: The report recorded that departmental or Ministry replies remained awaited at its cut-off. These are constitutional-auditor findings, not proof of bribery, fraud or personal criminal guilt, and no recovery or disciplinary outcome is inferred.

title: CAG finds waste-rule failures and unused landfill assets in Chandigarh
category: Environment, public health and municipal accountability
status: CAG compliance findings; current remediation not established
summary: CAG found failures to segregate waste, unscientific disposal of biodegradable waste and inadequate monitoring of ambient air and groundwater. It also classified Rs 2.99 crore spent on an unused sanitary landfill and leachate tank as infructuous expenditure and identified contract-supervision failures involving refuse-derived fuel.
outcome: The municipal corporation reported in 2016 that air and groundwater testing had begun, but CAG said that did not explain the long prior failure. The historical audit finding is not described as theft, corruption or proof that every defect persisted to 2026.

title: CAG finds Chandigarh Police recruitment money kept outside government accounts
summary: CAG found that Chandigarh Police conducted five recruitment exercises without submitting detailed grant demands to Parliament. Application fees were deposited in bank savings accounts and recruitment expenses were paid from them; the audit recorded receipts of about Rs 10.24 crore and expenditure of about Rs 1.25 crore through May 2016.
outcome: CAG held that the arrangement bypassed Article 266, parliamentary appropriation and financial rules. The record establishes unauthorized retention and expenditure as an audit finding, not embezzlement, personal enrichment or a criminal conviction.

title: Supreme Court restricts Phase I apartmentalisation in Chandigarh
category: Urban governance, housing and architectural heritage
status: Binding Supreme Court judgment and interim planning directions
summary: The Supreme Court held that the applicable rules prohibited fragmentation, division, bifurcation or apartmentalisation of residential units in Chandigarh's Phase I. It criticized authorities for sanctioning plans that effectively divided one dwelling into three apartments without adequately addressing heritage, traffic and parking consequences.
outcome: The Court required heritage and redensification review, Central approval for relevant planning changes and interim restrictions on disguised floor-wise division and plan sanction. The holding is limited to Phase I and is not presented as a ban on every apartment or co-ownership arrangement across Chandigarh.

title: Supreme Court reverses tampered Chandigarh mayoral result
status: Declared result quashed and corrected by Supreme Court
summary: After the 30 January 2024 mayoral vote, the presiding officer declared eight ballots invalid. Inspecting the ballots and video, the Supreme Court found that all eight were valid votes for Kuldeep Kumar and that the presiding officer had added marks to create a ground for rejection, altering the declared result.
outcome: The Court quashed the result, counted the eight ballots, produced a 20-16 tally and declared Kuldeep Kumar elected Mayor. It also ordered a Section 340 show-cause notice; that notice was not a criminal conviction. This was a municipal election, not a territorial Assembly contest.

title: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu merge into one Union Territory
summary: Parliament combined the two adjacent Union Territories through Act 44 of 2019. The law provided for continuity and transfer of laws, public employees, assets, liabilities and proceedings while preserving two Lok Sabha seats and Bombay High Court jurisdiction; Daman was designated headquarters through the related administrative process.
outcome: The two predecessor territories ceased separately and the merged Union Territory came into existence on 26 January 2020, with Praful Patel appointed Administrator from that date. The merger did not create statehood, a territorial legislature, a Chief Minister or a legislative opposition.

title: CAG identifies transport-enforcement and digital-control gaps in DNHDD
category: Road safety, regulatory enforcement and institutional accountability
status: Final CAG audit finding; corrective completion not established
summary: A CAG audit covering 2019-20 through 2023-24 found enforcement and control gaps involving registrations, permits, fitness certificates, fees, motor-vehicle tax, national permits, e-challans, pollution certificates and the VAHAN and SARATHI systems. The report also documented delays in public services and weaknesses in data-led monitoring.
outcome: The Department accepted several observations or promised corrective action, but the cited report did not establish complete post-audit closure. The findings are not evidence of corruption, criminal wrongdoing or particular road deaths.

title: CAG finds Rs 95.24 lakh avoidable electricity penalties at Daman hospital
status: Final CAG audit finding; official reply awaited at audit cut-off
summary: CAG found that Government Hospital, Daman paid Rs 95.24 lakh in avoidable penalties because electricity demand repeatedly exceeded the contracted level and the hospital did not revise that demand in time. The hospital said additional specialists, equipment and patient footfall had increased consumption and that it had begun seeking an enhancement.
outcome: CAG maintained that timely contract revision could have avoided the penalties. The audit does not establish embezzlement, bribery, deliberate loss, later recovery or disciplinary action.

title: CPCB classifies Damanganga stretch as Priority II polluted river
category: Environment, river pollution and regulatory performance
summary: CPCB reported monitoring the Damanganga at ten locations across Daman-Diu and Dadra and Nagar Haveli and found that six did not meet the prescribed biochemical-oxygen-demand criterion. It classified the stretch from Vapi Weir to Daman Jetty as a polluted river stretch in Priority Class II.
outcome: The assessment establishes non-compliant BOD at monitored locations and the official priority classification. It does not by itself identify a particular polluter, prove a specific health outcome or establish that cleanup was later completed.

title: Supreme Court clarifies Meghalaya coal-mining law after NGT prohibition
status: Legal regime clarified; restoration and enforcement remained continuing issues
summary: After the NGT directed that rat-hole and illegal coal mining stop, the Supreme Court held in 2019 that the central mining law applies in Meghalaya's Sixth Schedule areas. It preserved the prohibition on illegal and unregulated extraction while clarifying that lawful MMDR-compliant leases were not categorically barred.
outcome: The Court also directed transfer of Rs 100 crore for environmental restoration. CAG separately found that no coal-mining leases or licences had been issued during 2013-14 to 2017-18 and reported Rs 313.75 crore in unrealised royalty on coal stocks. These are judicial and audit findings, not personal criminal convictions.

title: Flooding at illegal Ksan coal mine traps 15 workers
category: Worker safety, illegal mining and disaster response
status: Fatal illegal-mining incident established; complete recovery not established
summary: Water entered a coal mine at Ksan in East Jaintia Hills and trapped 15 workers. The Supreme Court later treated the episode, using committee, FIR and magisterial material, as evidence that illegal coal mining continued after the NGT prohibition.
outcome: State and Union records later described all 15 workers as dead. This record does not claim that all bodies were recovered or assign personal criminal guilt beyond the findings of competent proceedings.

title: Mukroh border-area firing kills six
category: Interstate boundary, policing and public safety
status: Six deaths confirmed; responsibility and final boundary position unresolved
summary: A confrontation at Mukroh in the Assam-Meghalaya border area killed five Meghalaya civilians and one Assam forest guard. Meghalaya announced compensation and proposed a central-agency investigation; a judicial commission was also constituted.
outcome: The reviewed public record does not finally establish who initiated the confrontation or resolve the competing claims about the exact boundary location. Those questions are not converted into settled findings here.

title: Mynsngat-Thangsko illegal-mine explosion kills 27
category: Illegal mining, worker safety and enforcement
status: State-reported toll; judicial inquiry ongoing at the research cut-off
summary: The Meghalaya government's 9 February operational release reported that an explosion at an illegal coal mine in the Mynsngat-Thangsko area killed 27 people and injured nine. Earlier preliminary figures were lower; the later direct state count controls this entry.
outcome: A judicial inquiry headed by retired Justice R. S. Chauhan was still scheduling sittings and a site visit in July 2026. No final cause, individual liability or completed commission finding is asserted.

title: CAG finds exceptionally low state return from Mizoram lotteries
category: Public finance, state enterprises and institutional accountability
summary: For 2012-13 to 2014-15, CAG reported gross ticket sales of Rs 11,834.22 crore. Distributors deposited Rs 25.45 crore as minimum guaranteed revenue and administrative expenses; CAG calculated distributor accrual of Rs 2,182.79 crore while the state received 0.22% of gross sales.
outcome: CAG characterized the arrangement as revenue loss and undue benefit to distributors. Those are constitutional-auditor findings and are not relabelled as theft, bribery or a criminal conviction of a named person.

title: Bru policy shifts from limited repatriation to permanent Tripura settlement
status: Successive agreements signed; later implementation used revised beneficiary totals
summary: A 2018 four-party agreement aimed to repatriate 5,407 Bru families, comprising 32,876 people, from Tripura to Mizoram. After only limited return, a new agreement signed on 16 January 2020 provided permanent settlement in Tripura with land, housing and subsistence support.
outcome: The original announcement figures, later verified-beneficiary counts and final eligible-family totals are different dated snapshots. They are not merged into a false single number, and the 2018 plan is not described as fully implemented.

title: Fatal Assam-Mizoram boundary clash leaves seven dead
category: Interstate boundary, policing and federal relations
status: Deaths and Union review established; responsibility and boundary claim unresolved
summary: A violent confrontation near the Lailapur-Vairengte sector killed six Assam Police personnel and one civilian and injured more than 50 people. The Union Home Secretary reviewed the situation with both states on 28 July.
outcome: The state governments advanced conflicting accounts of first fire and the governing historical boundary. A 2024 joint statement recommitted both to peace and further talks, confirming that the wider dispute was not finally resolved.

title: CAG finds major gaps in Mizoram disaster preparedness
status: Final performance-audit findings tabled; later remediation not established
summary: CAG reported institutional and operational deficiencies including absent development-plan review for risk reduction, no State Advisory Committee meetings from 2017-18 through 2021-22, missing hazard-specific procedures, no automated landslide-monitoring system and no dedicated State Disaster Response Force battalion.
outcome: The audit covers historical periods spanning more than one ministry; it is not attributed solely to the government that tabled the report. The findings do not themselves establish personal criminal misconduct or that every deficiency persisted in July 2026.

title: Nagaland completes urban local-body election with women's reservation
category: Local democracy, women's representation and constitutional compliance
status: Election completed in participating bodies; 14 Eastern Nagaland councils did not participate
summary: After prolonged litigation and roughly two decades without urban local-body elections, Nagaland filled 278 wards in 25 participating bodies. The state compliance record accepted by the Supreme Court reported 103 women elected from 198 women candidates.
outcome: Fourteen town councils in Eastern Nagaland did not participate. The completed election therefore did not cover every urban local body and did not by itself resolve the political dispute behind the non-participation.

title: Oting and Mon security operation kills 14 civilians
category: Civilian deaths, security operations and accountability
status: State criminal proceedings closed for want of AFSPA sanction, subject to possible revival
summary: An Army operation and its aftermath near Oting and Tiru killed 13 civilians on 4 December; another civilian died in Mon town on 5 December. Nagaland's Assembly also recorded 35 civilians injured. The state SIT later charge-sheeted 30 personnel and awaited central prosecution sanction.
outcome: The Supreme Court recorded that sanction had been rejected and closed the proceedings arising from the FIRs, while permitting revival if sanction is later granted. The judgment did not decide the truth of the allegations or acquit personnel on the criminal merits.

title: AFSPA coverage is reduced in phases but remains in specified Nagaland areas
status: Partial territorial withdrawal; specified coverage continued at the research cut-off
summary: The Union government withdrew disturbed-area coverage from 15 police-station areas effective April 2022 and three more effective April 2023. The March 2026 notification continued AFSPA for six months from 1 April across specified whole districts and named police-station jurisdictions.
outcome: The policy is accurately described as phased territorial reduction, not statewide repeal. Nor did the 2026 notice apply to every part of Nagaland; its listed geographic scope controls.

title: Nagaland elects its first women MLAs
summary: Salhoutuonuo Kruse and Hekani Jakhalu became the first women elected to the Nagaland Legislative Assembly and took the oath on 20 March 2023. Kruse also entered the Council of Ministers as the state's first woman minister.
outcome: The record is limited to these Assembly and ministerial firsts. It does not erase earlier public or parliamentary service by Nagaland women or prove broader gender parity in state institutions.

title: Sikkim becomes India's first fully organic state
status: Statewide agricultural certification transition implemented and officially recognized
summary: A Government of India account states that Sikkim's entire agricultural area had been converted to certified organic in 2015 and that the state was formally declared India's first 100% organic state in 2016. The milestone was publicly marked at the Sikkim Organic Festival on 18 January.
outcome: The claim concerns certified agricultural area and the state policy transition. It does not mean that every product sold in Sikkim was organic or that certification eliminated every compliance problem.

title: Pakyong opens as Sikkim's first airport
summary: The Prime Minister inaugurated Pakyong Airport, identified in the official release as Sikkim's first airport and India's 100th airport under the UDAN regional-connectivity programme.
outcome: The inauguration created aviation infrastructure within Sikkim. It is not represented as proof of continuously uninterrupted commercial service, which later depended on weather and operating conditions.

title: Supreme Court expands Sikkim tax exemption and deletes disputed identity remarks
category: Rights, identity, taxation and constitutional law
status: Final judgment as corrected on review
summary: The Court held that excluding old Indian settlers domiciled in Sikkim before the 1975 merger from the Section 10(26AAA) exemption was discriminatory. It also struck down the proviso denying the exemption to a Sikkimese woman who married a non-Sikkimese after 1 April 2008.
outcome: After objections to language describing Sikkimese Nepalis, the Court deleted the disputed sentence and phrases on 8 February and clarified that Article 371F's validity or interpretation was not before it. The corrected record governs this entry.

title: South Lhonak glacial-lake outburst devastates the Teesta valley
category: Disaster, climate risk and critical infrastructure
status: Disaster established; reconstruction and GLOF mitigation continuing
summary: A glacial-lake outburst at South Lhonak sent a destructive flood down the Teesta, causing deaths and disappearances and damaging homes, roads, bridges and hydropower infrastructure, including the Teesta-III dam at Chungthang.
outcome: Official casualty snapshots changed rapidly during rescue operations, so this entry does not manufacture a single final death-and-missing total. Later official records consistently identify a South Lhonak GLOF rather than treating early cloudburst wording as the settled cause.

title: Courts invalidate Tripura's 10,323-teacher selection process
category: Public employment, education and judicial accountability
status: Appointments judicially invalidated; later employment consequences litigated
summary: The Tripura High Court set aside the challenged employment-policy provisions and the teacher selection, finding the process neither fair nor transparent. Later Supreme Court litigation recorded that the appointments of about 10,323 teachers had already been decided and that 8,882 ad hoc teachers remained at the end of the 2019-20 academic session before termination.
outcome: The Supreme Court preserved age relaxation for specified later recruitment exercises. The judgments address the policy and selection process; they do not establish that every affected teacher committed fraud, was personally culpable or was individually unqualified.

title: CAG finds systemic gaps in selected Tripura public hospitals
actors: Health and Family Welfare Department, Tripura
status: Final performance-audit findings tabled; later remediation not established
summary: For 2014-15 to 2018-19, CAG reported low availability of essential medicines, 139% bed occupancy at GB Pant Hospital, referral and leave-against-medical-advice pressures at sampled district hospitals, open biomedical-waste disposal and ambulances lacking basic life-support equipment.
outcome: CAG concluded that the audited district hospitals could not provide quality care for serious or major diseases, pushing patients toward the state hospital. The findings describe the audited period and sample; they are not criminal-guilt findings or a July 2026 snapshot of every facility.

title: Statewide Tripura floods and landslides kill 36
category: Disaster, agriculture, housing and relief finance
status: Later official scale established; additional central assistance approved
summary: After the entire state was declared a natural-calamity affected area, the later Union parliamentary assessment recorded 36 deaths, 3,584 cattle lost, 34,773 houses affected and 0.59 lakh hectares of cropped area affected.
outcome: Tripura sought Rs 7,080.55 crore, while the Union High-Level Committee later approved Rs 288.93 crore in additional NDRF assistance subject to the stated SDRF-balance adjustment. The request, assessed losses and approved assistance are not the same figure.

title: NGT finds major gaps in Lakshadweep waste compliance
category: Environment, waste management and institutional compliance
status: Tribunal findings and remedial directions; later complete compliance not established
summary: In the Lakshadweep portion of the national solid-waste proceeding, the NGT found that required Solid Waste Management Rules steps were incomplete and that action on plastic and biomedical waste was inadequate, leaving a major gap between the measures taken and legal requirements.
outcome: The Tribunal directed personal monthly monitoring, a six-month compliance effort, periodic reporting, environmental-restoration valuation, performance audit and a monitoring cell. Continued filings prove ongoing oversight, not that every deficiency either persisted unchanged or was fully cured.

title: New Lakshadweep panchayat regime operates through Special Officers
status: New regulation in force; general election blocked and bodies administered by appointed officers at cut-off
summary: The Lakshadweep Panchayat Regulation, 2022 replaced the 1994 regime and ten Dweep Panchayats plus one District Panchayat were notified. The Administration's July 2026 page says a pending Kerala High Court case prevented a general election and that Special Officers were administering the bodies.
outcome: This is the Administration's official current explanation and operating status. It does not establish that elections were permanently abolished or adjudicate the merits and responsibility in the pending litigation.

title: Low-temperature thermal desalination expands to eight Lakshadweep islands
category: Drinking water, island infrastructure and technology
status: Eight named island plants reported operational by March 2026
summary: Union records traced expansion from demonstration plants at Kavaratti, Agatti and Minicoy to additional systems. By March 2026, operational plants were reported on Kavaratti, Minicoy, Agatti, Amini, Kalpeni, Kadmat, Chetlat and Kiltan.
outcome: The record supports eight operational islands, not all ten inhabited islands. Androth was not in the cited operational list, and proposed ocean-thermal-energy projects are not counted as commissioned desalination capacity.

title: Submarine fibre and solar-storage systems are dedicated in Lakshadweep
category: Digital connectivity, energy and island infrastructure
status: Commissioned infrastructure with later telecom status reporting
summary: The Kochi-Lakshadweep Islands submarine optical-fibre project connected 11 islands and official material reported bandwidth increasing from 1.7 Gbps to 200 Gbps. Kavaratti and Agatti solar-storage installations together were reported at 1.7 MW of solar capacity and 1.4 MWh of battery storage.
outcome: The project assets were dedicated on 3 January 2024. Conflicting official estimates of the fibre route length are omitted, and forecast diesel, financial and emissions savings are not presented as measured outcomes.

title: Supreme Court upholds central nomination power and full voting rights
summary: The Supreme Court held that nomination of Puducherry Assembly members under the Government of Union Territories Act is Central Government business and did not legally require consultation with the local Council of Ministers. It also held that nominated members may vote on all Assembly questions.
outcome: The ruling covers budget and no-confidence votes as well as ordinary questions. Nominated members remain nominated, not elected, and the Court's legal-authority ruling did not make a finding about the political neutrality of the selections.

title: Puducherry ministry falls, central rule follows, and elected government returns
summary: After resignations and a prior disqualification reduced the government side, the Speaker declared the 22 February confidence motion lost and Narayanasamy's resignation was accepted effective that day. President's Rule began on 25 February, followed by an election and Rangasamy's oath on 7 May.
outcome: AINRC won ten elected seats and BJP six, giving the incoming coalition a 16-of-30 elected majority. The three nominated members were appointed on 10 May, after the ministry formed, and are not used to manufacture the initial majority.

title: CAG finds systemic deficiencies in Puducherry urban water supply
category: Drinking water, urban services and institutional accountability
status: Final compliance-audit findings tabled; later remediation not established
summary: CAG found no comprehensive water-supply plan, avoidable delay across all 14 sampled works, inadequate rainwater-harvesting enforcement, non-cleaning and non-chlorination concerns, high TDS and chloride, and Rs 49.44 crore in water-charge arrears whose age profile was not known to the department.
outcome: The audit also recorded an undue benefit to a purified-water contractor. That phrase is CAG's audit conclusion; it is not converted into a judicial corruption finding or criminal conviction.

title: Cyclone Fengal makes landfall near Puducherry amid record rainfall
category: Cyclone, extreme rainfall and urban flooding
status: Landfall and extreme rainfall established; final impact total not asserted
summary: Cyclone Fengal crossed the north Tamil Nadu-Puducherry coast close to Puducherry late on 30 November. MHA recorded 490 mm of rain in 24 hours at Puducherry, while IMD's monsoon report lists 49 cm on 1 December and categorizes it as torrential.
outcome: A central team was deployed to assess damage. Early situation reports were evolving operational snapshots, so this entry does not manufacture a single final death, displacement or loss total without a reconciled final memorandum.

title: Ladakh forms and begins staffing five new districts
actors: Residents of Nubra, Sham, Changthang, Zanskar and Drass
status: Districts formed and initial district officers appointed
summary: The Ladakh administration formed five new districts—Nubra, Sham, Changthang, Zanskar and Drass—on 27 April 2026. The next day the Lieutenant Governor approved assignments of Deputy Commissioners and Superintendents of Police so the new administrative structures could begin functioning.
outcome: The appointments establish initial operationalisation of the five districts. They do not by themselves prove that every department, office, budget or public service was fully transferred and functioning on the formation date.

title: Cyclone Hudhud crosses Visakhapatnam as a very severe cyclonic storm
category: Cyclone, infrastructure damage and disaster response
status: Landfall and meteorological intensity established; recovery followed
summary: IMD recorded Hudhud crossing the north Andhra Pradesh coast over Visakhapatnam on 12 October with maximum sustained winds of about 185 km/h, severe damage to structures, trees, communications and electricity systems, and a storm surge of about 1.4 metres.
outcome: This entry uses the reconciled meteorological record and omits casualty and monetary-loss totals that vary across later assessments. It does not treat forecast warnings, immediate estimates and final loss accounting as interchangeable.

title: LG Polymers styrene leak kills residents around Visakhapatnam plant
category: Industrial safety, environment and corporate accountability
actors: Residents of R. R. Venkatapuram and surrounding communities
status: Industrial disaster occurred; compensation and legal proceedings followed
summary: A styrene leak from LG Polymers' Visakhapatnam facility affected surrounding communities. The Environment Ministry report filed before the NGT recorded 12 reported deaths and about 4,000 affected people at that stage.
outcome: The NGT applied strict environmental-liability principles, required an interim Rs 50 crore deposit and directed restoration and compensation processes. Those directions and dated impact figures are not converted into a final criminal conviction of every officer or executive.

title: Manohar International Airport opens as Goa's second airport
category: Aviation, public infrastructure and transport capacity
status: Licensed, inaugurated and opened for commercial operations
summary: The Mopa aerodrome was licensed on 26 October 2022, inaugurated as Manohar International Airport on 11 December and received its first commercial flight on 5 January 2023. Phase I was designed for about 4.4 million passengers annually.
outcome: The official record establishes a two-airport system for Goa and distinguishes licensing, inauguration and commercial opening. Design capacity is not presented as achieved annual traffic, and the stated project benefits are not treated as measured outcomes.

title: CAG identifies staffing, ICU, procurement and infrastructure gaps in Goa health services
category: Public health, staffing, procurement and audit accountability
status: Final performance-audit findings tabled; later remediation not inferred
summary: For the audited period 2016-17 to 2021-22, CAG reported 753 vacancies across doctors, nurses, paramedics and other staff at Goa Medical College and Hospital as of 31 March 2022, no ICU services in either district hospital during the period, and procurement and infrastructure deficiencies.
outcome: The report also recorded that a first Rs 8.40 crore trauma-care instalment released in March 2018 remained untransferred after more than four years. These are attributed audit findings across several administrations, not criminal convictions or proof that every deficiency persisted unchanged through 2026.

title: Extremely severe Cyclone Fani crosses Odisha near Puri
category: Cyclone, housing, infrastructure and disaster recovery
status: Landfall and state damage assessment established; reconstruction followed
summary: IMD recorded Extremely Severe Cyclonic Storm Fani crossing the Odisha coast near Puri on 3 May 2019. The state later reported about 5.6 lakh damaged houses and extensive damage to power, water, roads, schools, health facilities, crops and livelihoods.
outcome: Odisha's official assessment placed public-property losses and prompt relief needs at about Rs 9,336 crore and requested about Rs 5,228 crore from the NDRF under the applicable norms. The assessed loss, state request and any later sanctioned assistance are kept as different figures.

title: Punjab floods affect all 23 districts and more than 2,400 villages
category: Flood, agriculture, housing and disaster response
actors: Army, Air Force and disaster-response agencies
status: Official situation-report scale established; relief and reconstruction continued
summary: An MHA disaster situation report recorded floods across all 23 Punjab districts after heavy rain, rising Ravi and Beas levels and dam releases. It listed 2,431 affected villages, 389,042 affected people, 23,340 rescues or evacuations and 10,216 damaged houses at that reporting point.
outcome: The same operational report recorded 58 deaths, five missing people and about 198,596 hectares of affected agricultural land. Later official summaries used different dates, definitions and totals, so this entry keeps the situation report's snapshot rather than manufacturing a single final toll.

title: Harda fireworks-factory explosions kill 13 and damage nearby homes
category: Industrial safety, explosives regulation and compensation
actors: National Green Tribunal and National Human Rights Commission
status: Disaster and official impact record established; compensation oversight continued
summary: A series of explosions at a fireworks factory near Harda affected workers and surrounding residents. The district administration's status, later recorded by the NGT, listed 13 deaths, 64 grievous injuries, 156 simple injuries, 39 burned or damaged houses and 201 evacuated houses.
outcome: NHRC sought a state report on responsibility and recurrence prevention, while NGT proceedings tracked victims, damage and compensation. Commission notices and tribunal supervision are not converted into final criminal convictions or personal culpability findings.

title: Acute Encephalitis Syndrome emergency kills children in Muzaffarpur
category: Public health, child mortality, surveillance and health-system response
actors: Children and families in Muzaffarpur district
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
status: Dated official response and reported toll snapshots established; single final causal account not asserted
summary: During the June 2019 Acute Encephalitis Syndrome emergency, NHRC's 17 June notice cited reports of 100 child deaths by that date. As the reported toll evolved, official agencies deployed pediatric teams, ambulances and surveillance measures and reviewed clinical, nutritional and epidemiological evidence.
outcome: The Union Health Ministry documented active case finding, daily surveillance and multidisciplinary investigation. A dated reported toll is not presented as a final reconciled total, and the evidence does not support reducing every case to one food, one cause or one administrative act.

title: Tabrez Ansari killing leads to ten trial-court convictions
category: Mob violence, criminal justice and appellate accountability
status: Ten trial-court convictions and sentences established; current appellate finality not asserted
summary: Tabrez Ansari was assaulted by a mob in Saraikela-Kharsawan district in June 2019 and later died. In June 2023 a trial court convicted ten accused under IPC section 304 and related provisions; on 5 July it imposed ten-year rigorous-imprisonment sentences.
outcome: The entry records the trial-court disposition, not a finally affirmed appellate result. It does not assign guilt to acquitted or untried people, and no claim is made about the current status of any appeal without a later court order.
